$comment: The canonical protected_source_category vocabulary -- the protocol's FIRST producer<->consumer conformance boundary. A TWO-LEVEL scheme: a COARSE required category (the consumer's execution-flow bucket, what phase-loop keys its protected-source handling on) plus an OPTIONAL fine `subtype` (the producer's governance granularity, what governed-pipeline emits). Both governed-pipeline (producer) and phase-loop (consumer) validate against THIS distributed registry -- they conform to the contract, not to each other, so the vocabulary is never defined implicitly in a codebase (the 'conform to each other, not the contract' anti-pattern the open protocol exists to eliminate). SoT ONLY at this version: gp emits coarse+subtype and phase-loop validates against `coarse_categories` in LATER phases (PSCAT-GP / PSCAT-PL); this version neither changes gp's emit nor phase-loop's accept. WHY coarse is the hard contract and subtype is free-form: the coarse enum is the cross-repo agreement both runtimes gate on; enum-gating the FINE level in the distributed schema would re-couple the fine granularity across repos -- the exact anti-pattern this boundary avoids. The registered fine set below is therefore a DATA map (fine -> coarse), not a schema-enforced enum; a later boundary validator MAY check an emitted subtype against `fine_subtypes` as a soft/warn signal, never a schema hard-fail. Resolves #155; unblocks the R2 bake (#146).

coarse.description: The execution-flow bucket the consumer (phase-loop) keys its protected-source handling on. Every emitted protected_source_category MUST carry one of these coarse ids.

subtype.description: The producer's (governed-pipeline's) fine governance granularity, carried alongside the coarse category. Free-form at the schema level (any string) so the fine vocabulary is NOT re-coupled across repos; the `fine_subtypes` registered set + `fine_to_coarse` map below are the canonical fine names for tooling/diagnostics, checkable by a later validator as a soft signal only.

coarse_categories.description: Canonical specification sources and their archive/mirror manifests -- the spec surface the loop treats as protected input.
Diagram sources treated as protected input to the loop.
Runtime adapter configuration and execution profiles that parameterize how the loop runs.
Pipeline/definition source files (e.g. the pipeline definition JSON and the code that validates it).
Portal-facing contract surfaces the loop treats as protected input.
Per-phase artifacts (current-phase source bundle) the loop protects across iterations.

NEW coarse bucket (0.6.5) for producer-side governance sources that do not map onto any of the six execution-flow buckets above: adoption contracts, the phase-loop bridge contract files, skill manifests, and instruction surfaces. These are governance-of-the-loop inputs rather than spec/definition/adapter/portal/phase-artifact inputs; the panel chose a SINGLE minimal coarse bucket (rather than splitting) and lets `subtype` carry the fine distinction, keeping coarse growth minimal.
